ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for pickled mustard with shredded pork; mapped to broader 'pork dish' class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for pickled mustard with shredded pork; A0CKP (pork meat dish) used as the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of pickled mustard with shredded pork as a side dish at a Singaporean meal is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a small bowl or a generous side portion alongside rice.

nutrient_source.title: Pickled Mustard with Shredded Pork — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 酸菜 Pickled Mustard With Shredded Pork (cr_id G00010049). All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat is not separately reported by HPB for this entry; estimated at 3.5g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central pork loin stir-fry data (NDB 10219) as a secondary reference for fatty acid breakdown.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates
gi_source.source_match_notes: Carbohydrate content is 0.0g/100g per HPB source. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by calculation (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving / 100 = not applicable; reported as 0.0). University of Sydney GI Database consulted; no GI entry exists for this dish, consistent with its negligible carbohydrate content.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, rinse pickled mustard greens thoroughly before cooking to reduce sodium, avoid additional soy sauce or salty condiments, and restrict portion to no more than 50 g per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate content is favourable for glycaemic control, but high fat (10.5 g/100g) and very high sodium (1050 mg/100g) are secondary concerns
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is low-carbohydrate and does not raise blood glucose directly; however, the high fat content from pork may impair insulin sensitivity over time, and the very high sodium load is an independent cardiovascular risk factor in people with Type 2 Diabetes — limit portion size to 100 g and pair with high-fibre vegetables.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g combined with cholesterol 89 mg/100g and saturated fat from pork elevates cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of this dish due to its very high sodium content (1050 mg/100g), which raises blood pressure, combined with dietary cholesterol (89 mg/100g) and saturated fat from pork; if consumed, limit to a small portion (50 g) and balance with potassium-rich vegetables.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6) amplifying inflammatory cytokines; sodium 1050 mg/100g worsens tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish due to pork's pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid load and the extremely high sodium content from pickled mustard greens; consider substituting pork with tofu or chicken and rinsing pickled greens thoroughly to reduce sodium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pickled Mustard with Shredded Pork (酸菜炒肉丝, Suān cài chǎo ròu sī) is a classic Chinese-Singaporean home-style stir-fry dish featuring fermented and pickled mustard greens (酸菜, suān cài) cooked together with thin strips of tender pork loin or belly. The pickled mustard greens are characteristically sour, crunchy, and intensely savoury, having been preserved in salt and brine, which gives the dish its distinctive tangy flavour profile. The shredded pork is typically marinated in soy sauce, sesame oil, and cornstarch before being stir-fried at high heat, resulting in tender, juicy strips that contrast with the crunch of the pickled greens. Garlic and sometimes chilli are added for aromatic depth. The dish is served as a side dish alongside steamed white rice in a typical Singaporean Chinese meal at lunch or dinner. Nutritionally, it is high in protein (19.1 g/100g) and moderate in fat (10.5 g/100g), with negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g/100g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. However, its sodium content is very high at 1050 mg/100g due to the pickling process and soy sauce seasoning, making it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease without significant portion restriction. Cholesterol is 89 mg/100g from the pork. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those following halal dietary requirements. It contains soy allergens from soy sauce.

Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g/100g per HPB); gi_category set to 'not_applicable'. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific pickled mustard with shredded pork preparation are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry G00010049, and no equivalent breakdown is available in USDA FoodData Central for this exact composite dish as of 2026; both approved sources were consulted and confirmed unavailability of these specific nutrient fractions for this dish. saturated_fat_g estimated at 3.5 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central pork loin stir-fry reference (NDB 10219) as the closest approved-source equivalent; this is an adaptation and not directly reported by HPB.
